canonical_definition: MetricX is consistently characterized across the provided literature as an automatic metric for machine translation (MT) evaluation. Its primary application is to measure 'Translation quality', and it is also used as a baseline for score prediction. The provided sources describe it as a 'state-of-the-art' metric, with one paper noting it was the 'winner of the WMT’24 Metrics Shared Task' ("From Jack of All Trades to Master of One: Specializing LLM-based Autoraters to a Test Set"). While its status as a top-performing metric is a recurring theme, a less common description focuses on its architecture. One paper specifies that MetricX is 'built on mT5' and 'adopts a regression-based framework' to predict translation quality by learning from human judgments ("CAFE: Retrieval Head-based Coarse-to-Fine Information Seeking to Enhance Multi-DocumentQACapability").
papers: CAFE: Retrieval Head-based Coarse-to-Fine Information Seeking to Enhance Multi-DocumentQACapability
DetectingLLMHallucination Through Layer-wise Information Deficiency: Analysis of Ambiguous Prompts and Unanswerable Questions
Enhancing Large Vision-Language Models with Ultra-Detailed Image Caption Generation
UnpackingLet Alone: Human-Scale Models Generalize to a Rare Construction in Form but not Meaning
From Jack of All Trades to Master of One: Specializing LLM-based Autoraters to a Test Set
Overestimation in LLM Evaluation: A Controlled Large-Scale Study on Data Contamination’s Impact on Machine Translation

definitions.text: A state-of-the-art automatic machine translation evaluation metric used as a baseline for score prediction.
